text: The surgeon performs a careful digital examination to assess the severity of anal stenosis. An SNM retractor is inserted, followed by an incision of fibrotic tissue at the three o'clock position in the anal canal. This incision provides a clear view of the area for flap positioning. The mucosa of the lower rectum is gently detached, exposing healthy internal tissue for a safe suture with a cutaneous flap.

The surgeon marks a rhomboid shape on the skin to define the flap size, followed by a cutaneous incision using a cold knife scalpel. The subcutaneous tissue is incised with diathermy, ensuring a conic shape by making the flap's base larger than its surface. Care is taken to preserve the blood supply and avoid damaging the superficial layer of the external sphincter during the preparation of the flap's medial part.

The surgeon transposes the flap to the top of the anal canal and sutures it to the healthy mucosa using long-term absorbable stitches. The donor site is closed, ensuring no dead space or tension on the internal suture. The skin is closed, and the diameter and elasticity of the anal canal are evaluated to determine the need for a contralateral flap.
